intro: Theft — the wrongful taking of another's property — is forbidden by the eighth commandment (Ex. 20:15; Deut. 5:19) and regulated in detail throughout Mosaic law. The Law required restitution proportional to the goods stolen: a thief who slaughtered or sold a stolen ox must restore five oxen; a sheep, four (Ex. 22:1–4). Stealing persons (kidnapping) carried the death penalty (Ex. 21:16; Deut. 24:7). The prophets condemned economic theft broadly — false weights, fraudulent measures, and oppression of laborers (Amos 8:5; Mic. 6:11; Lev. 19:35–36). The wisdom literature states plainly: "Do not steal" (Prov. 22:22–23) and notes that poverty does not justify it (Prov. 6:30–31). In the NT, Jesus lists theft among the vices that defile a person from within (Mark 7:21–22). Paul commands the former thief to labor honestly and share with those in need rather than steal (Eph. 4:28). Jesus himself uses the imagery of a thief's unexpected arrival to urge readiness for his return (Matt. 24:43; Rev. 3:3; 16:15).
